claims.claim: Quantized key-value (KV) caches reduce memory bandwidth and capacity requirements during autoregressive transformer inference, but introduce approximation error that degrades accuracy at very low bit widths.

claims.claim: Using a synthetic associative KV-cache retrieval task implemented in NumPy, we compare full-precision training against training with 2-bit and 3-bit per-vector symmetric quantization of the KV cache, using the straight-through estimator for gradients.

claims.claim: Across five random seeds, 2-bit cache-QAT recovers a mean accuracy improvement of +2.95 percentage points (SE ≈ 0.90 pp) at 2-bit deployment relative to a full-precision-trained baseline, with all five seeds showing positive deltas.

evidence_refs.quote: Quantized key-value (KV) caches reduce memory bandwidth and capacity requirements during autoregressive transformer inference, but introduce approximation error that degrades accuracy at very low bit widths. We investigate whether incorporating cache quantization into the training forward pass—cache quantization-aware training (cache-QAT)—can mitigate this degradation at deployment time. claims.claim: A natural question is whether training with quantized caches in the forward pass can prepare the model for the distortions it will encounter at deployment.

evidence_refs.quote: # Cache Quantization-Aware Training: A Synthetic Feasibility Study

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We pose the following research question: **Can training with a quantized KV cache in the forward pass improve inference accuracy when the deployed KV cache is quantized?** To isolate the effect of cache quantization from the many confounding factors in a full LLM pipeline, we design a minimal synthetic associative KV-cache retrieval task.
